questions.text: I would decline an award or honor and redirect attention to the team.
I sense when someone is withholding information based on who else is present.
I thrive when I have the authority to make things happen.
I pick up on tension between colleagues even when they are being perfectly professional.
I would give up a cherished personal goal if doing so served a greater collective need.
I find that one strong emotion can completely hijack my attention.
I have been told that I am a great team player.
I don't tend to carry the emotional residue of one event into the next.
I tend to miss when a conversation has a dual purpose beyond its surface topic.
I find that emotional recovery is a slow process for me compared to others.
I have a hard time following when I think I could lead better.
My sleep schedule shifts depending on what I'm engaged with; I follow my energy.
I use tools like spreadsheets, project boards, or apps to keep my life organized.
I think that effective leadership requires the ability to make decisions about people's lives without being swayed by their individual stories.
I believe that suffering creates a moral obligation that is more compelling than any argument against acting.
I find that the felt weight of suffering is the experience that most consistently reminds me that my ethical responsibilities extend beyond my immediate circle.
I find that advance planning allows me to be more present during execution.
I tend to allocate the majority of my resources to my own development.
